strategy: agentic chunking via Gemini (headline + summary + original text)

pages.text:
3

What is good posture?
Many people have some idea about what perfect posture is. You probably associate it
with military posture. What most people cannot do, is precisely define why one posture
is better than another. Kritz & Cronin (2008) define perfect posture as follows:
"Optimal standing static posture is when the least amount of neuromuscular activity is
required to maintain body position in space and that which minimizes gravity stresses
on the body. The biomechanical rationale for achieving and maintaining optimal posture
is to move efficiently, free of impairment and dysfunction."

Perfect posture?

Popular theory in physical therapy states that postural changes occur concurrently with
an imbalance of the associated musculature. Certain muscles get strengthened or
weakened over time, which leads to a different ratio in strength or flexibility of the
agonist (the muscle providing force during a movement) and the antagonist (the muscle
opposing the force) muscles acting over a joint. The agonist muscle group gets strong
and tight while the antagonist muscle group gets weak and lengthened. Causality can
run in both directions, but the common theory is that strong or overactive muscles pull
your posture in a certain direction, away from the weak or lengthened muscles, and
posture is thus the result of your muscular balance.

4

Upper and lower crossed syndrome
2 Of the most common postural profiles are upper and lower crossed syndrome.
• Lower crossed syndrome is anterior tilt of the pelvis and hyperlordosis in the
lumbar spine ('belfie posture'). See the left image below.
• Upper crossed syndrome is forward head positioning and forward shoulder
rounding. See the right image below.

The hypothesis of upper and lower crossed syndrome.

It's worth noting that research finds no relation between forward head posture and
kyphosis (upper spine flexion).

While superficially plausible, there are many problems with the prevailing view of
optimal posture.

5

Short = strong; long = weak?
The common theory, as illustrated above, is that an overactive muscle is short, tight and
strong and an underactive muscle is long, flexible and weak.

Yet the length-tension relation of muscle contraction strength supports muscles do not
become stronger when they shorten: they become weaker as the actin and myosin
filaments overlap too much for maximum force production. Maximum total force
production generally occurs when a muscle is slightly lengthened due to increased
passive force production, so if the pecs would shorten and the scapular retractors would
lengthen, this could actually increase scapular retraction force. The muscle strength
ratio theory of posture is thus not in line with basic biomechanics. Likewise, the ratio of
spinal extension to flexion strength increases with spinal flexion, so if resting posture
were determined by muscle tension ratios, kyphosis should self-correct, not worsen
over time.

Moreover, as you learned in the course module on stretching, strength training normally
results in either no change in muscle length or an increase, not a decrease, again
showing a disconnect between muscle length and strength.

The scarce research directly examining posture in relation to muscle length and
strength is conflicting. For example, Cools et al. (2010) found that elite tennis players
have more scapular upward rotation in their dominant sides and this was associated
with shortened pectoralis minor length. Since the pectoralis minor is a downward rotator
of the shoulder blade, you would expect it to lengthen, not shorten, as the scapula's
resting posture rotated upward. Serratus anterior strength was greater on the dominant
side though, supporting a relation between upward rotation strength and posture. It's
tempting to conclude a lot of upward rotation during exercise causes the resting position
of the scapula to rotate upwardly accordingly, but other research finds that overhead
athletes show downward rotation of the scapula of the dominant arm [2]. So there
headings: athletes show downward rotation of the scapula of the dominant arm [2]. So there

pages.text:
6

doesn't appear to be a straightforward relationship between the type of movement a
joint regularly performs and its resting posture.

Tight = short?
We are intuitively inclined to conclude a muscle is short when we feel it is tight.
However, the sensation of tightness is just that: a feeling. What we experience as
muscle tightness is often a signal from your muscle (spindles) to your brain to contract a
muscle or prevent it from further stretching. It does not necessarily mean that a muscle
is actually short. Consider the following scenarios you've already seen when you
generally feel tightness.
• DOMS often makes a muscle feel stiff and tight without any change in its length.
• Heavy eccentric exercise often makes a muscle feel tight while this actually
provides a stimulus to lengthen the muscle.
• Stretching reduces the sensation of muscle tightness without any change in its
resting length.

Many people also experience stiffness when they have a fever and sometimes stiffness
can be purely psychological. In short, you cannot conclude that a muscle is short
because it feels stiff.

Postural assessment
How do you diagnose someone's posture? Well, you can just look at it, right? You
surely get some idea of someone's posture this way, but this idea is more about
someone's body shape than someone's internal joint positions. It's like looking at a
house to determine the structure of the foundation. That may be somewhat accurate for
very simple box style houses, but when a house has a more complex shape, you can no
longer reliably determine its internal structure from its outside appearance.

7

Let's take anterior tilt, for example. Many people are worried they have too much
anterior tilt in their pelvis, which makes their butt and belly stick out, AKA Donald Duck
syndrome.

Anterior tilt (left; 'Donald Duck syndrome') vs. posterior pelvic tilt (right; during a squat
this is often informally called 'buttwink')

When you look at someone's body from the side, you can only get a very rough idea of
their pelvic tilt angle. If someone has serious booty and a bit of a pot belly, they may
appear to have anterior pelvic tilt even with a neutral pelvis. So many people infer the
angle of the pelvis by the angle of the spine. While you often move your spine and
pelvis together, posturally speaking, the measured angle of the pelvis and spine are
poorly correlated [2].

So researchers have come up with a method, using something called the ASIS-PSIS
angle of tilt, to determine someone's pelvic position based on its bony landmarks rather
than just visual appearance (illustrated below, but you don't have to understand the
method in depth).

8

Schematic diagram of the pelvis illustrating the ASIS-PSIS measure of pelvic tilt and the
ischial spine-pubic symphysis measure of tilt. The ASIS-PSIS measure is defined as the
angle between the horizontal and a line drawn between the ASIS and the PSIS. The
ischial spine-pubic symphysis measure is defined as the angle between the horizontal
and a line drawn between the ischial spine and the pubic symphysis.

However, even this method is very fallible, because we humans don't just differ in pelvic
tilt angle but also pelvic shape. Variations in pelvic shape can make it appear
someone's in anterior pelvic tilt when they're actually not. See the example below for 2
pelvises positioned in their neutral position. The left one actually appears neutral and
comes out of the test as such with a 0° ASIS-PSIS angle; however, the right one has a
massive 23° ASIS-PSIS angle, which would register as major anterior tilt.
So if you were to 'fix' this person's pelvic tilt, you'd actually put them in major buttwink
and if they'd use that posture for things like squatting and deadlifting, they'd most likely
get injured.

9

Even when you've actually measured someone's posture with reliable methodology,
such as an MRI scan, that's just 1 snapshot in time. Basically every time we stand, we
stand slightly and sometimes very differently. There is major variability in standing
posture not only between but also within individuals: this applies to everyone regardless
of whether they're involved in exercise or whether they have pain. Think of how you
stand when you just get out of bed, versus how you stand when you're public speaking,
versus how you stood when your teacher berated you in front of the whole class.

Our posture is significantly related to our psychological state, perhaps more so than our
muscular state. For example, we intuitively stand up taller when we feel pride, and we
slump more when we feel disappointment.

Pathology vs. variation
Let's take postural assessment a step further and say you have diagnosed that
someone has a true postural deviation like anterior pelvic tilt based on an MRI scan.
Common theory is that you should correct this, because it's a pathology.

10

A pathology, from Greek pathos "suffering" + logia "study", by definition requires
symptoms like pain or dysfunction. However, Herrington (2011) found that many people
with anterior pelvic tilt do not have any symptoms and many other studies, including a
2008 systematic review, have found no relation between lower back pain and spinal
posture, and minimal relation between spinal range of motion and incidence of low back
pain [2, 3, 4, 5].

Even gross anatomical variations like leg length asymmetry are not consistently
associated with low back pain.

Pregnancy can increase lordosis and anterior pelvic tilt in the third trimester, but this is
again not correlated with low back pain.

In fact, a 2019 systematic review of systematic reviews found no consistent relation
between any type of posture and low back pain.

Another systematic review of longitudinal studies found no relations between back,
shoulder or neck pain and muscle strength, balance, endurance or range of motion.

For the shoulder too posture and pain are often unrelated, yet physical therapists tend
to diagnose postural abnormalities in the shoulder more often when they know someone
has shoulder pain. The same physical therapists in that study correctly diagnosed no
differences when they were unaware of the presence of pain. In other words, physical
therapists tend to be biased - as we all are - to see imaginary problems that fit the
falsified theory they've been taught.

Not only are postural deviations not commonly associated with pain, they may be
beneficial for athletes.
headings: between any type of posture and low back pain.

pages.text:
11

Watson (1993) found that athletes involved in running sports such as soccer and
football show marked anterior pelvic tilt (lordosis) compared to other sportsmen. He also
studied the posture of 11 soccer players over 3 years and found the lordosis of 8 of
them to be significantly increased with their participation in the sport. Anterior pelvic tilt
may be biomechanically beneficial for horizontal force production.

Upper-crossed syndrome also does not seem to be inherently associated with any
adverse physical effects. Many boxers and swimmers have forward rounded shoulders
and kyphosis (thoracic flexion) without any corresponding issues; in fact, this posture
may be advantageous to increase stroke rate in the water according to Bloomfield
(1998).

It thus seems that the diagnosis of anterior pelvic tilt is often based on abnormality per
se, not the presence of any actual problems and sometimes even when the abnormality
may be advantageous. It's a sad state of affairs if we inherently equate 'not normal' with
'bad'.

And is military posture really normal in the first place? In Herrington's study, 85% of
males and 75% of females had an average of 6-7°of anterior pelvic tilt. In the same
study, 6% of males and 7% of females exhibited posterior pelvic tilt, while only 9% of
males and 18% of females showed a neutral hip position. And again, none of these
individuals had any symptoms. Let's emphasize this: the majority of the asymptomatic
population 'suffered' from anterior pelvic tilt. So we can't even say anterior pelvic tilt is
abnormal in the first place.

It's well established in the literature that very few people are happy with their posture.
For example, Korakakis et al. (2020) found that in a sample of 100 young,
asymptomatic individuals, none of them thought they sat in an optimal posture. But if
none of them had any problems from their posture, what exactly was wrong with it?
Sitting posture is not associated with injury rates.
headings: individuals had any symptoms. Let’s emphasize this: the majority of the asymptomatic

pages.text:
12

And remember the course section on structural asymmetries: humans are not perfectly
symmetrical. Knutson (2005) found that 90% of people had a leg length inequality of 5.2
mm (± 4.1). A difference in how long your legs are is generally not considered a clinical
concern up to 2 cm (0.78").

Similarly, many team sports athletes have significantly asymmetrical strength and
performance development as a result of their sport's movement requirements; this
asymmetry is not associated with impaired performance.

It is time to abandon the textbook myth that all humans should have a certain structure
and posture. Humans are natural organisms, not robots produced in a factory. The
human motor cortex can achieve full functionality with a wide range of structures and
postures. Unless you actually have symptoms, there's little reason to go looking for
problems. Trying to fix what isn't broken can do more harm than good.

Postural behavior
Resting posture can vary significantly between individuals without any corresponding
problems, but what about active posture? The physical therapy wisdom is that
prolonged sitting shortens the hamstrings and hip flexors and deactivates the glutes,
causing anterior pelvic tilt, lordosis and back pain.

Yet Hartvigsen et al. (2000) found that sitting at work was no more associated with low
back pain than other postures, like standing, driving or bending. Our posture while
sitting or standing - slumped or upright - also doesn't predict the likelihood we'll get
back pain. Several other review papers also reached the conclusion that pain and
resting posture are not significantly related to our day to day postural behavior [1, 2, 3,
4].

13

Another systematic review of prospective cohort studies from 2009 "found strong
evidence that leisure time sport or exercises, sitting, and prolonged standing/walking
are not associated with low back pain. Evidence for associations in leisure time
activities (e.g., do-it-yourself home repair, gardening), whole-body vibration, nursing
tasks, heavy physical work, and working with one's trunk in a bent and/or twisted
position and low back pain was conflicting."

We do know that simple overuse can cause back pain, but that's a far cry from the
theory that your posture changes your body structure and causes pain or dysfunction.

We can take things another step further and try to diagnose not just static and active
posture but also active movement. The Functional Movement Screen (FMS) has
become very popular to assess if your body is moving as it should. Based on a series of
bodyweight stretches and exercises, it diagnoses imbalances.

Sounds great… doesn't work. The FMS score is barely more accurate than flipping a
coin at predicting injury risk [2, 3, 4] or performance. Movement is a skill, and a highly
specific one. So how someone moves on a few rudimentary tests or what their resting

14

posture is says very little about how they move during their sport. You can see
gymnasts at the Olympics sitting on the bench with horribly slouched posture before
they win a medal. A world-class powerlifter may pick up his gym bag with a majorly
rounded over spine yet deadlift 800 pounds with a neutral spine.

Postural correction
Suppose you do have an actual problem, i.e. pain or dysfunction, and you associate this
with a postural abnormality. What should you do?

Let's start with a more basic question: can you change your resting posture with
exercise in the first place? A big problem with measuring resting posture is what
position 'resting posture' really is. How you stand at one point in time is not necessarily
the same as at a different time, as anyone with experience measuring their waist
circumference can tell you. A slumped posture may not indicate a muscular problem but
rather depression; in fact, research shows you can induce a slumped posture in people
by having them listen to sad music. Since exercise increases wellbeing and reduces
anxiety, any kind of exercise program may result in people standing more upright by
virtue of increased confidence, without any necessary relation with muscular balance.
Simply knowing you're trying to correct your posture may also have you subconsciously
change your resting posture.

If we assume resting posture doesn't change over time due to other factors though, it
appears that exercise can indeed change resting posture.
• Strengthening exercises over an 8-week program have been shown to decrease
forward head and shoulder posture in swimmers compared to a control group.
• Falla et al. (2007) found that a neck strengthening group could over time
maintain a more upright posture during prolonged sitting than a control group.

15

• In this case study, a 28-year-old man with excessive lordosis and severe lower
back pain improved his anterior pelvic tilt angle from 20 degrees to 14 degrees,
increased range of motion of the lumbar spine and decreased lower back pain.
• In another case study, a 37-year-old man with flat back (posterior pelvic tilt) and
lower back pain completed a similar 2-week strengthening protocol and
increased his anterior pelvic tilt angle, increased lumbar range of motion and
decreased lower back pain.

However, Wang et al. (1999) found no effect of pectoral stretching and upper back
training on resting scapular position in individuals with forward shoulder posture.
Thoracic extension did increase after the training program.

Stretching programs appear to be effective as well.
• A 2-week pectoral stretching program has been found to decrease forward
shoulder posture compared to a control group.
• Harman et al. (2005) found that after a 10-week progressive pec stretching and
shoulder retracting program the experimental group had significantly less
distance between their scapulae, i.e. their shoulders were "pulled" back more
during resting posture.

However, the effect of stretching may be short-lived. A study with 55 adults showed that
a hamstring stretching protocol led to an acute improvement of anterior pelvic tilt and
greater lumbar flexion, but Borman et al. (2011) found that a 4-week hamstring
stretching program had no effect on lumbar flexion range of motion and lumbar
curvature, even though hamstring muscle extensibility increased.

Importantly, the above studies are all on individuals with significant postural
abnormalities and non-strength trained muscles. This begs the question if the problem
is muscular balance or just outright weakness. A study in healthy individuals by Lin &
Karduna (2016) found no effect of rotator cuff and scapular strengthening exercises on

16

scapular kinematics (how the shoulder blade moved during exercise), suggesting we
can't fix what isn't broken.

And none of these studies show that one type of exercise is more effective than
another. Nourbakhsh & Arab (2002) found that poor muscle endurance and weakness
are associated with low back pain but that structural factors such as the magnitude of
the lumbar lordosis, pelvic tilt, leg length discrepancy, and the length of abdominal,
hamstring, and iliopsoas muscles are not relevant, suggesting that exercise in itself is
the key, not muscular balance.

Lee et al. (2017) compared 3 different exercise programs for the correction of rounded
shoulder posture. All programs were equally effective, suggesting the benefits come
from activity itself, not which exercises you do specifically.

One of the few studies of combined stretching and strength training came to a similar
conclusion: There was no significant corrective benefit of adding a stretching program to
a strength training program in women with severely abnormal shoulder posture. There
was a trend for the combined therapy group to experience better outcomes, but the
finding that even in entirely untrained people with severe shoulder abnormalities
stretching may not have any added benefit to very moderate strength training makes the
relevance of stretching highly questionable for someone that engages in strength
training with full range of motion exercise.

In support of the primary importance of exercise, regardless of type, most studies of
exercise, regardless of type, have noted an overall reduction in back pain intensity,
ranging from 10% to 50% after exercise treatment. Another review likewise concluded:
"There is strong evidence stabilization exercises are not more effective than any other
form of active exercise in the long term." The literature about neck pain comes to similar
conclusions.

17

Since the relation between muscle strength, flexibility and endurance and pain are all
weak and many types of exercise seem to be effective with little difference in how
effective various exercise forms are, functionality does not seem to be the primary
mechanism of action of why exercise reduces pain and injury risk. Rather, it may simply
be that increased blood flow and tissue turnover are good for tissue maintenance. There
is no evidence that exercise specifically targeting muscular balance is any more
effective to either improve posture or decrease symptoms than a general full-body
strength training protocol. And there is a complete lack of evidence that a healthy
strength trainee can further improve his or her posture with specific exercises.

You know what probably does work to change your posture? Changing your posture. If
you want to walk with your shoulders further back, pull your shoulders back. If you want
to keep your chest up more, keep your chest up. Nothing prevents you from doing so.
Posture is essentially a habit. Changing habits is difficult and takes time, but it can be as
simple as willing it.

18

Take-home messages
• There is no such thing as a universally perfect posture. Humans vary significantly
in structure and many different types of posture can be functional.
• You cannot accurately diagnose a postural abnormality from the outside.
• A single snapshot of someone's posture is not necessarily representative of their
regular posture.
• Neither resting nor active posture are inherently associated with any adverse
physical symptoms.
• The Functional Movement Screen similarly has poor accuracy at predicting injury
risk or performance. Each exercise's technique should thus be evaluated
independently in a strength training program.
• Worry first and foremost about your symptoms, namely pain and functionality.
Don't go looking for problems or try to fix what isn't broken.
• In so far as you need to change your posture with exercise at all, the type of
exercise may not matter. When you're already training your full body with full
range of motion strength exercises, it is highly questionable you can meaningfully
change your resting posture or body structure by targeting your muscular
balance.

19

Learning objectives
After having studied this module, you should be able to do the following.
• Explain why anterior tilt is not necessarily a problem that needs to be fixed with
corrective exercise.
• Critique the prevailing theory that a joint's resting position is primarily influenced
by the ratio of muscle strength and length around the joint.
• Explain why someone can score poorly on a Functional Movement Screen yet be
entirely pain-free and highly functional.
• Name the 2 primary diagnostic criteria to differentiate between abnormal and
pathological posture.

➢ Click here to try a mock exam for this module

What is good posture?
Many people have some idea about what perfect posture is. You probably associate it
with military posture. What most people cannot do, is precisely define why one posture
is better than another. Kritz & Cronin (2008) define perfect posture as follows:
"Optimal standing static posture is when the least amount of neuromuscular activity is
required to maintain body position in space and that which minimizes gravity stresses

The common theory, as illustrated above, is that an overactive muscle is short, tight and

--- Page 6 ---
6

doesn't appear to be a straightforward relationship between the type of movement a
joint regularly performs and its resting posture.

We are intuitively inclined to conclude a muscle is short when we feel it is tight.
However, the sensation of tightness is just that: a feeling. What we experience as
muscle tightness is often a signal from your muscle (spindles) to your brain to contract a
muscle or prevent it from further stretching. It does not necessarily mean that a muscle
is actually short. Consider the following scenarios you've already seen when you
generally feel tightness.
• DOMS often makes a muscle feel stiff and tight without any change in its length.
• Heavy eccentric exercise often makes a muscle feel tight while this actually
provides a stimulus to lengthen the muscle.

Many people also experience stiffness when they have a fever and sometimes stiffness
can be purely psychological. In short, you cannot conclude that a muscle is short
because it feels stiff.

How do you diagnose someone's posture? Well, you can just look at it, right? You
surely get some idea of someone's posture this way, but this idea is more about
someone's body shape than someone's internal joint positions. It's like looking at a
house to determine the structure of the foundation. That may be somewhat accurate for

--- Page 7 ---
7

syndrome.

Anterior tilt (left; 'Donald Duck syndrome') vs. posterior pelvic tilt (right; during a squat
this is often informally called 'buttwink')

their pelvic tilt angle. If someone has serious booty and a bit of a pot belly, they may

angle between the horizontal and a line drawn between the ASIS and the PSIS. The

and a line drawn between the ischial spine and the pubic symphysis.

Our posture is significantly related to our psychological state, perhaps more so than our
muscular state. For example, we intuitively stand up taller when we feel pride, and we
slump more when we feel disappointment.

Let's take postural assessment a step further and say you have diagnosed that
someone has a true postural deviation like anterior pelvic tilt based on an MRI scan.
Common theory is that you should correct this, because it's a pathology.

to diagnose postural abnormalities in the shoulder more often when they know someone
has shoulder pain. The same physical therapists in that study correctly diagnosed no
differences when they were unaware of the presence of pain. In other words, physical
therapists tend to be biased - as we all are - to see imaginary problems that fit the
falsified theory they've been taught.

Not only are postural deviations not commonly associated with pain, they may be
beneficial for athletes.


--- Page 11 ---
11

Watson (1993) found that athletes involved in running sports such as soccer and
football show marked anterior pelvic tilt (lordosis) compared to other sportsmen. He also
studied the posture of 11 soccer players over 3 years and found the lordosis of 8 of
them to be significantly increased with their participation in the sport. Anterior pelvic tilt
may be biomechanically beneficial for horizontal force production.

Upper-crossed syndrome also does not seem to be inherently associated with any
adverse physical effects. Many boxers and swimmers have forward rounded shoulders
and kyphosis (thoracic flexion) without any corresponding issues; in fact, this posture
may be advantageous to increase stroke rate in the water according to Bloomfield
(1998).

It thus seems that the diagnosis of anterior pelvic tilt is often based on abnormality per
se, not the presence of any actual problems and sometimes even when the abnormality
may be advantageous. It's a sad state of affairs if we inherently equate 'not normal' with
'bad'.

And is military posture really normal in the first place? In Herrington's study, 85% of
males and 75% of females had an average of 6-7°of anterior pelvic tilt. In the same
study, 6% of males and 7% of females exhibited posterior pelvic tilt, while only 9% of
males and 18% of females showed a neutral hip position. And again, none of these
individuals had any symptoms. Let's emphasize this: the majority of the asymptomatic
population 'suffered' from anterior pelvic tilt. So we can't even say anterior pelvic tilt is
abnormal in the first place.

It's well established in the literature that very few people are happy with their posture.
For example, Korakakis et al. (2020) found that in a sample of 100 young,
asymptomatic individuals, none of them thought they sat in an optimal posture. But if
none of them had any problems from their posture, what exactly was wrong with it?
Sitting posture is not associated with injury rates.


--- Page 12 ---
12

And remember the course section on structural asymmetries: humans are not perfectly
symmetrical. Knutson (2005) found that 90% of people had a leg length inequality of 5.2
mm (± 4.1). A difference in how long your legs are is generally not considered a clinical
concern up to 2 cm (0.78").

Similarly, many team sports athletes have significantly asymmetrical strength and
performance development as a result of their sport's movement requirements; this
asymmetry is not associated with impaired performance.

It is time to abandon the textbook myth that all humans should have a certain structure
and posture. Humans are natural organisms, not robots produced in a factory. The
human motor cortex can achieve full functionality with a wide range of structures and
postures. Unless you actually have symptoms, there's little reason to go looking for
problems. Trying to fix what isn't broken can do more harm than good.

Postural behavior
Resting posture can vary significantly between individuals without any corresponding
problems, but what about active posture? The physical therapy wisdom is that
prolonged sitting shortens the hamstrings and hip flexors and deactivates the glutes,
causing anterior pelvic tilt, lordosis and back pain.

Yet Hartvigsen et al. (2000) found that sitting at work was no more associated with low
back pain than other postures, like standing, driving or bending. Our posture while
sitting or standing - slumped or upright - also doesn't predict the likelihood we'll get
back pain. Several other review papers also reached the conclusion that pain and
resting posture are not significantly related to our day to day postural behavior [1, 2, 3,
4].


--- Page 13 ---
13

Another systematic review of prospective cohort studies from 2009 "found strong
evidence that leisure time sport or exercises, sitting, and prolonged standing/walking
are not associated with low back pain. Evidence for associations in leisure time
activities (e.g., do-it-yourself home repair, gardening), whole-body vibration, nursing
tasks, heavy physical work, and working with one's trunk in a bent and/or twisted
position and low back pain was conflicting."

We do know that simple overuse can cause back pain, but that's a far cry from the
theory that your posture changes your body structure and causes pain or dysfunction.

We can take things another step further and try to diagnose not just static and active
posture but also active movement. The Functional Movement Screen (FMS) has
become very popular to assess if your body is moving as it should. Based on a series of
bodyweight stretches and exercises, it diagnoses imbalances.

Sounds great… doesn't work. The FMS score is barely more accurate than flipping a
coin at predicting injury risk [2, 3, 4] or performance. Movement is a skill, and a highly
specific one. So how someone moves on a few rudimentary tests or what their resting


--- Page 14 ---
14

posture is says very little about how they move during their sport. You can see
gymnasts at the Olympics sitting on the bench with horribly slouched posture before
they win a medal. A world-class powerlifter may pick up his gym bag with a majorly
rounded over spine yet deadlift 800 pounds with a neutral spine.

Postural correction
Suppose you do have an actual problem, i.e. pain or dysfunction, and you associate this
with a postural abnormality. What should you do?

Let's start with a more basic question: can you change your resting posture with
exercise in the first place? A big problem with measuring resting posture is what
position 'resting posture' really is. How you stand at one point in time is not necessarily
the same as at a different time, as anyone with experience measuring their waist
circumference can tell you. A slumped posture may not indicate a muscular problem but
rather depression; in fact, research shows you can induce a slumped posture in people
by having them listen to sad music. Since exercise increases wellbeing and reduces
anxiety, any kind of exercise program may result in people standing more upright by
virtue of increased confidence, without any necessary relation with muscular balance.
Simply knowing you're trying to correct your posture may also have you subconsciously
change your resting posture.

If we assume resting posture doesn't change over time due to other factors though, it
appears that exercise can indeed change resting posture.
• Strengthening exercises over an 8-week program have been shown to decrease
forward head and shoulder posture in swimmers compared to a control group.
• Falla et al. (2007) found that a neck strengthening group could over time
maintain a more upright posture during prolonged sitting than a control group.


--- Page 15 ---
15

• In this case study, a 28-year-old man with excessive lordosis and severe lower
back pain improved his anterior pelvic tilt angle from 20 degrees to 14 degrees,
increased range of motion of the lumbar spine and decreased lower back pain.
• In another case study, a 37-year-old man with flat back (posterior pelvic tilt) and
lower back pain completed a similar 2-week strengthening protocol and
increased his anterior pelvic tilt angle, increased lumbar range of motion and
decreased lower back pain.

However, Wang et al. (1999) found no effect of pectoral stretching and upper back
training on resting scapular position in individuals with forward shoulder posture.
Thoracic extension did increase after the training program.

Stretching programs appear to be effective as well.
• A 2-week pectoral stretching program has been found to decrease forward
shoulder posture compared to a control group.
• Harman et al. (2005) found that after a 10-week progressive pec stretching and
shoulder retracting program the experimental group had significantly less
distance between their scapulae, i.e. their shoulders were "pulled" back more
during resting posture.

However, the effect of stretching may be short-lived. A study with 55 adults showed that
a hamstring stretching protocol led to an acute improvement of anterior pelvic tilt and
greater lumbar flexion, but Borman et al. (2011) found that a 4-week hamstring
stretching program had no effect on lumbar flexion range of motion and lumbar
curvature, even though hamstring muscle extensibility increased.

Importantly, the above studies are all on individuals with significant postural
abnormalities and non-strength trained muscles. This begs the question if the problem
is muscular balance or just outright weakness. A study in healthy individuals by Lin &
Karduna (2016) found no effect of rotator cuff and scapular strengthening exercises on


--- Page 16 ---
16

scapular kinematics (how the shoulder blade moved during exercise), suggesting we
can't fix what isn't broken.

And none of these studies show that one type of exercise is more effective than
another. Nourbakhsh & Arab (2002) found that poor muscle endurance and weakness
are associated with low back pain but that structural factors such as the magnitude of
the lumbar lordosis, pelvic tilt, leg length discrepancy, and the length of abdominal,
hamstring, and iliopsoas muscles are not relevant, suggesting that exercise in itself is
the key, not muscular balance.

Lee et al. (2017) compared 3 different exercise programs for the correction of rounded
shoulder posture. All programs were equally effective, suggesting the benefits come
from activity itself, not which exercises you do specifically.

One of the few studies of combined stretching and strength training came to a similar
conclusion: There was no significant corrective benefit of adding a stretching program to
a strength training program in women with severely abnormal shoulder posture. There
was a trend for the combined therapy group to experience better outcomes, but the
finding that even in entirely untrained people with severe shoulder abnormalities
stretching may not have any added benefit to very moderate strength training makes the
relevance of stretching highly questionable for someone that engages in strength
training with full range of motion exercise.

In support of the primary importance of exercise, regardless of type, most studies of
exercise, regardless of type, have noted an overall reduction in back pain intensity,
ranging from 10% to 50% after exercise treatment. Another review likewise concluded:
"There is strong evidence stabilization exercises are not more effective than any other
form of active exercise in the long term." The literature about neck pain comes to similar
conclusions.


--- Page 17 ---
17

Since the relation between muscle strength, flexibility and endurance and pain are all
weak and many types of exercise seem to be effective with little difference in how
effective various exercise forms are, functionality does not seem to be the primary
mechanism of action of why exercise reduces pain and injury risk. Rather, it may simply
be that increased blood flow and tissue turnover are good for tissue maintenance. There
is no evidence that exercise specifically targeting muscular balance is any more
effective to either improve posture or decrease symptoms than a general full-body
strength training protocol. And there is a complete lack of evidence that a healthy
strength trainee can further improve his or her posture with specific exercises.

You know what probably does work to change your posture? Changing your posture. If
you want to walk with your shoulders further back, pull your shoulders back. If you want
to keep your chest up more, keep your chest up. Nothing prevents you from doing so.
Posture is essentially a habit. Changing habits is difficult and takes time, but it can be as
simple as willing it.


--- Page 18 ---
18

Take-home messages
• There is no such thing as a universally perfect posture. Humans vary significantly
in structure and many different types of posture can be functional.
• You cannot accurately diagnose a postural abnormality from the outside.
• A single snapshot of someone's posture is not necessarily representative of their
regular posture.
• Neither resting nor active posture are inherently associated with any adverse
physical symptoms.
• The Functional Movement Screen similarly has poor accuracy at predicting injury
risk or performance. Each exercise's technique should thus be evaluated
independently in a strength training program.
• Worry first and foremost about your symptoms, namely pain and functionality.
Don't go looking for problems or try to fix what isn't broken.
• In so far as you need to change your posture with exercise at all, the type of
exercise may not matter. When you're already training your full body with full
range of motion strength exercises, it is highly questionable you can meaningfully
change your resting posture or body structure by targeting your muscular
balance.


--- Page 19 ---
19

Learning objectives
After having studied this module, you should be able to do the following.
• Explain why anterior tilt is not necessarily a problem that needs to be fixed with
corrective exercise.
• Critique the prevailing theory that a joint's resting position is primarily influenced
by the ratio of muscle strength and length around the joint.
• Explain why someone can score poorly on a Functional Movement Screen yet be
entirely pain-free and highly functional.
• Name the 2 primary diagnostic criteria to differentiate between abnormal and
pathological posture.
